statement: AI Chat Builder surface (seed) — AI Chat is seeded as a future Builder capability, not a full AI runtime in MVP. It gives users a branded, in-canvas assistant entry point that will later help generate and structure DCX content. — AI entry point lives in the Kanban Builder Island. Clicking opens a branded sticky popup (resizable, minimizable, sticky on canvas) using the shared popup shell. Future capabilities: read proposal, extract phases/actions/tasks, break tasks into subtasks, assign channels, complete structure from objectives/templates/strategy metadata. AI output must never directly mutate Builder data; it must go through review/preview/apply + normal validation.

Flexible AI/context metadata fields (seed) — The data model should seed future AI support by allowing hidden/flexible context fields on key entities, without polluting core structured fields. — Allow optional context fields (e.g. strategy_context, generation_context, ai_context, metadata, source_context) on DCX, Version, Phase, Action, Task, Subtask. These are additive, never replace core fields, and are for future AI context, generation source, proposal/strategy links, reasoning, and template/objective mapping.
Default creation view — Kanban is the default Builder creation view. — StageManager, KanbanStage, BuilderKanbanView, view initialization.
Last opened view behavior — If the user previously opened the same version, restore the last opened — Local preference store, builder initialization, view switcher.
Kanban creation scope — User can create phases, actions, and tasks in Kanban. — BuilderKanbanView, HorizontalBoard, KanbanColumn, phase/action/task cards, builderMutations.
Phase creation — User can create phases directly in Kanban. — Phase card, add phase action, useBuilderNodes, cmdCreatePhaselater.
Action creation — User can create actions inside a phase in Kanban. — KanbanColumn, action container/card, cmdCreateAction.
Task creation — User can create tasks inside an action in Kanban. — Task card, action container, TaskEditor, cmdCreateTask, task factory.
Timeline creation scope — Timeline allows task creation only, not phase/action creation as a main workflow. — TimelineStage, BuilderTimelineView, DayGridCard, task creation handlers.
Timeline day task creation — User can create a task from inside a day in Timeline. The — DayGridCard, date helpers, task factory, timeline task creation
Timeline task parent selection — A timeline-created task must be nested under an existing phase/action or create — Timeline quick-create UI, parent selector, quick phase/action creation,
Minimal timeline task data — Timeline quick-create can add minimal task data first, then user completes details — Task factory, minimal task defaults, TaskEditor, validation badges.
Full task configuration location — Full task configuration happens in the Editor Island / TaskEditor. — TaskEditor, Editor Island, task card edit actions, validation.
Creator Island role — Creator/Builder Island supports guided creation, shortcuts, and future workflow helpers, but Kanban — CreatorIsland, ViewHelperIsland, useDeliverableWizard, island config.
View-specific creation tools — Creation tools should respect the current view’s capabilities. — Island config, view capability matrix, StageManager.
Import location — Import should exist for offline updates/recovery but live quietly inside Project Metadata — Project Metadata Island, import modal, backup import utility.
Import purpose — Import supports offline updates, JSON backup recovery, and controlled structure restoration. — JSON import parser, validation, reconciliation preview, local backup
Import mode — Import should support selective reconciliation rather than blind full replacement. — Import reconciliation engine, preview UI, mapper layer, validation
Stable IDs for import — Supabase-backed entities must have stable IDs from phase to subtask. — Supabase schema, API DTOs, JSON backup schema, import
Matching ID behavior — If an imported entity ID matches an existing entity ID, update/overwrite it — Import reconciler, update mapper, preview summary.
New ID behavior — If imported data contains new IDs, confirm that new items will be — Import preview modal, add operation mapping.
Missing ID behavior — If existing IDs are missing from the imported file, alert the user — Import diff engine, delete operation mapping, confirmation modal.
Import preview groups — Import preview should group changes into updated, added, and deleted/missing items. — Import preview UI, diff summary, readable payload formatter.
Validation after import — Imported structure must be validated before applying or before allowing Ready. — Import validator, schema validation, readiness validation, error reporting.
Template system readiness — Import/reuse logic should be modular because later users will save/import phase, action, — Import/export engine, future template DTOs, Kanban Builder Island.
Template DB scope — Template database for phase/action/task components is not MVP, but should be anticipated. — Future Supabase tables, template service, import engine compatibility.
Save as template entry — Future “save as template” action should likely live in Kanban Builder Island — Kanban Builder Island, phase/action/task menus, template service later.
Files support creation — Approved files/docs support the ICS while building but do not define the — File Manager Island, Project Metadata/File Island, local file
Active file state — Active/opened files are local user workflow state, not shared backend version state. — Local UI state, File Manager Island, no backend
Task cannot be orphaned — All creation/import flows must preserve the rule that tasks belong to actions. — Creation functions, import reconciler, validation, API schema.
Empty planning containers — Phases/actions can be created empty during planning but must be completed before — Phase/action creation, validation, status transition.

Creation operations should map to named commands — Creation actions should eventually route through named commands like create phase/action/task rather — Future builderMutations.ts, useBuilderNodes, builderStore.
Creation behavior events — Creation actions should be trackable later as meaningful behavior events, not as — Event adapter, analytics event names, no required MVP

Monthly calendar view layout & interaction — In the Builder's monthly Timeline view: (a) NO vertical scroll — all month day cards fit in one compact 7-column grid on screen (refer to v0.1.4 docs/archive/dcx-manager-v0.1.4/src/pages/builder/timeline/MonthlyView.tsx for the clean monthly grid); (b) day cards are compact (more compact than weekly); (c) the task-card EXPANDED state is DISABLED in monthly — tasks render only as small compact cards; (d) editing a task is via long-click on the small task card, which opens the Task Editor; (e) opening the editor must NOT push the day cards off-screen — the day grid shrinks further so the whole month stays visible while the editor is open. Current MonthlyView uses overflow-y-auto (vertical scroll) — this requirement supersedes that.

Weekly calendar view layout — In the Builder's weekly Timeline view, the stage fits exactly 5 expanded weekday cards (Mon–Fri) + 2 collapsed weekend day cards (Sat, Sun) within the visible stage, with NO horizontal scroll across the desktop viewport range (per REQ-RESP-001, 14in→85in). Weekdays render expanded; weekend days render collapsed/compact. Day cards follow the unified ~80%-height/10%-margin model (CC-2). Replaces phase columns in this view per REQ-STG-001.
Activity log storage — Activity logs are stored in Supabase as structured records. — Supabase activity_logstable, lifecycle services, version transition handlers.
MVP log scope — MVP activity logs cover lifecycle events only. — Version lifecycle service, status transition commands.
Logged lifecycle events — Log version created, moved to In Progress, Ready for Approval, Approved, Superseded, — Lifecycle mutation handlers, import/duplicate service.
Log visibility in MVP — Latest logs are shown from DB but manually synced/loaded, not real-time. — Project Metadata Island / Version History area, manual
Meaningful change logs — Seed architecture for meaningful structure-change logs later. — Event/log adapter, mutation command names, optional future change_logs.
Error report channel — Error reports can be sent through lightweight email/free integration. — Error reporter service, email/webhook integration, failed action capture.
Error report payload — Error reports include readable failed payload/context when relevant. — Payload formatter, error boundary/report modal, API error handler.
User-triggered reporting — User should have a report/retry path when critical save/load/import fails. — Error UI, retry action, report action.
Separation from audit logs — UX behavior tracking is separate from business activity logs. — Lightweight event layer, future analytics adapter.
Avoid noisy writes — Do not write logs for every UI interaction or autosave. — Event filtering, lifecycle-only DB writes.

Builder card density model — across the desktop viewport range (REQ-RESP-001), the Builder must comfortably fit, without premature overflow/scroll: (a) ~3 expanded phase columns in the stage (per core.md §21 1440 density); (b) ~3 collapsed action cards within an expanded phase card's action area; (c) ~3–4 collapsed task tiles within an action card's task row. Overflow beyond these targets is SIGNALED (OA-1 gradient fades) but the default density must be MET by compact card spacing — without breaking consistent spacing / visual rhythm across Phase/Action/Task card types. Known gap (PO-observed 2026-06-30): 3 collapsed action cards do NOT currently fit comfortably in a phase → drives a compact action-card design (sprint CC-7).
Official DCX structure — Official saved structure is Version → Phase → Action → Task. — types/domain.ts, types.ts, api.ts, BuilderNode, node.helpers.ts, api-mappers.ts.
Subtask role — Subtasks are saved inside tasks but are task details/configuration, not a major — Task type, TaskEditor, task factory, validation, task card
Task parent rule — A task cannot exist without an action. — Task creation flows, CreatorIsland, builderMutations, validation, API payload.
Empty phases/actions while planning — Empty phases/actions may exist while planning, but they cannot pass “Ready” validation. — Validation layer, status transition logic, phase/action cards, Ready
Day role — Day is not a primary saved entity. It is calculated from task — Timeline helpers, WeeklyView, MonthlyView, DayGridCard, date helpers.
Version communication date — Version has a communication date, but it can also be To Be — Version creation form, version type, builder loader, date
Communication date as anchor — The version communication date acts as the anchor for linked task dates. — Task date model, date helpers, timeline rendering, save
TBD communication date behavior — If communication date is TBD, linked task dates stay as relative offsets — TaskDate, date resolver, validation, display state.
Task date modes — Task date is stored as a mode object: unset, linked, or fixed. — TaskDate type, api.ts, domain.ts, TaskEditor date controls, mappers.
Linked task date — Linked task date stores relative position from communication date, not the final — Date resolver, mapper layer, timeline views, validation.
Fixed/custom task date — If a task is unlinked, it becomes a fixed/custom calendar date and — Task date object, TaskEditor, date display logic, validation.
Resolved date strategy — Resolved calendar dates are calculated when needed, not stored as the source — Date helpers, timeline helpers, mappers, display components.
Communication date update — Updating/adding communication date recalculates linked task dates dynamically. It should not be — Date resolver, version update logic, timeline refresh, save
Fixed date exception — Fixed/custom task dates are excluded from communication-date shifting. — Date resolver, TaskEditor, validation.
Action date span — Action start/end dates are calculated from the earliest/latest task dates inside the — date.helpers.ts, timelineHelpers.ts, action cards, mappers.
Phase date span — Phase start/end dates are calculated from the earliest/latest task/action dates inside the — Phase cards, timeline, date helpers, mapper layer.
Stored vs calculated fields — Task dates are source of truth. Action/phase spans may be cached for — Backend DTOs, API mappers, date helpers, timeline views.
Backend naming — Backend should use clean domain naming like actions[]; builder UI can map — api.ts, api-mappers.ts, node.helpers.ts, types/domain.ts.
API/domain/builder mappers — API, domain/app model, and builder render model should be separated through mapper — api-mappers.ts, node.helpers.ts, BuilderNode, service layer.
Builder nodes — BuilderNode[] / PhaseNode is a render model for the builder stage, not — builder-node.types.ts, useBuilderNodes.ts, node.helpers.ts.
UI state exclusion — Selection, hover, expanded cards, island state, active popover, active file preview, and — Builder store, island hooks, local storage if needed,
Timeline/day views — Timeline, weekly, monthly, and day views are generated from task dates. They — Timeline views, day cards, date helpers.
Missing data and specs field model — For missing data and specs fields only, task is ready when each — TaskEditor fields, task type, validation, readiness checker, API
Centralized date logic — Date calculations should live in shared helpers, not scattered across cards/islands/timeline components. — date.helpers.ts, timelineHelpers.ts, TaskEditor date controls.
Stage movement, scroll direction, and dropzone behavior are recovered from the prior implementation.

Typed shared dropzone engine — Dropzones should be generated by view rules, not hardcoded inside components, so Kanban and Timeline drag/drop stay consistent and future-proof. — A typed dropzone model defines: view type, target type, accepted dragged object types, target ID, position, active state, edge-zone state, and the command to run on drop. Dropzones are generated by view rules. Drop actions route through named mutation commands.

Embedded Google Docs/file preview — Version-level Drive/Docs links should be previewable inside the Builder using the shared popup shell, so users can reference approved docs without leaving the canvas. — Attempt to render the document via iframe/embed where supported, inside the shared sticky popup shell. If embedding fails (permissions, link format, browser restrictions), show a fallback to open externally. Rules: do not store file content, do not parse file content as product data in MVP, do not mutate Builder data from preview, Google Drive permissions remain outside the app.

Editor/Viewer Island (stage-pushing deep editor) — The current Task Editor should become a general Editor/Viewer Island — the controlled deep-editing surface for complex object forms — that pushes the stage rather than overlapping it. — MVP use: Task Editor. Future use: phase details, action details, AI metadata, strategy fields, advanced object forms. It is NOT a floating popup: it pushes/reduces the main stage width instead of overlapping the canvas. While open, the stage stays usable, cards stay selectable, the user can navigate the reduced stage, and the opened object stays selected/highlighted. Not resizable, but may contain smaller internal popups. On close/switch/leave with unsaved changes, the app must ask to save, discard, or cancel.

Focus Island as selection/filtering engine — Focus Island is a strategic selection/filtering engine, not a simple filter. It lets users find and concentrate on specific work across the whole plan while preserving hierarchy. — Supports focus modes by Week, Action, Task, and Task property/spec (sender, receiver, channel, task type, subtask, future structured fields). On selecting a focus option, the system finds all matching tasks, selects them, highlights with animation, expands any collapsed phase/action containing them, and preserves hierarchy context. Works in both Kanban and Timeline.

Focus isolation (visual hide only) — Users should be able to isolate selected work by hiding everything else from view, without ever deleting data. — Focus Isolation Mode hides all non-selected tasks from the current view. An action with no visible selected tasks is hidden; a phase with no visible selected tasks is hidden. This is visual filtering only — no data is deleted. Works in Kanban and Timeline.
File ownership level — Files are attached at the version level, not DCX level. — Version model, file metadata table, version creation payload,
New DCX file attachment — When a new DCX is created from the homepage, the system automatically — Homepage DCX creation, version creation service, project payload,
File storage type — MVP stores file links/references, mostly Google Drive links, not uploaded file content. — File metadata schema: name, URL, source, createdBy, versionId.
Version-only file scope — Files are not attached to phases/actions/tasks in MVP. Version-level only. — File Manager Island, file schema, no task attachment
Active file viewing state — Opened file, active preview, selected file, tab state, zoom, and similar viewer — File Manager Island, local UI preferences, no backend
File access follows version access — Anyone who can access the version can see the version’s file links. — Access guard, version file query, file link display.
Add a live Version-readiness summary in the header / metadata island, updating on every edit. The "stats" surface the discovery missed.
SMART default: active phase(s) expanded, the rest collapsed columns. Scales to 7–8 phases without a wall of scroll.
AUTO-CENTRE on select/navigate: selecting a card or jumping via focus/filter scrolls the stage to centre it (object-specific centring).
Drag-to-create pill: dragging from KBI-001 button creates a new card. Existing REQ-KBI-001 captures this; refined by PO alignment from core-interaction-model.

Task card as a single responsive component that resizes between collapsed/expanded (not separate components). Spacing, margins, font-sizes must be responsive/token-driven. Requires design-exploration step + PO sign-off before implementation.
FocusIsland DEFAULT mode = highlight/spotlight: activates/highlights matching values while keeping non-matching VISIBLE. No opt-in isolation mode. Full picture retained at all times.
ViewHelperIsland is NOT absent — code is view-gated (only renders in timeline/calendar views). Confirmed in scope; wire browser-verification step for timeline view in the relevant sprint.
Wire entry points so AIChatPopup and TemplatePopup are reachable. Both in scope for v0.3.5.
Theme toggle must actually switch dataset.theme/classList. PREREQUISITE for all light-theme token work (L01–L08) — must land before/with the change-token light-theme sprint.
Dedicated sprint to add reduced-motion branches across effects.registry.ts + islands/cards per FP-R4 M01–M05 and core.md §20.
Homepage/version reference available at docs/archive/dcx-manager-v0.1.4 (full prior codebase). FP-R5 grounds homepage/version sprints in this reference + brand contract.
Brandbook values extracted from PDF text layer. Use output/brandbook-values.md as the FP-R1 values source.
Enable click-to-open on collapsed editor when a card is selected (in addition to drag).
Routing & Endpoint Directory fields: single-column layout (full width). No editor-width change.
Collapsed Phase readiness text via tooltip (hover) + aria-label (screen readers).
Tokenize layout widths/heights (phase 72/260px, editor 382px, header 64px, footer 76px). Same pixels, sourced from tokens, not hardcoded. Aligns with D-01 responsive direction.

During requirements-system rollout, the graph must operate in test/calibration mode: strict architecture remains binding, while imperfect mappings may remain provisional when visible, auditable, reversible, and queued for cleanup.

Agents may use graph data as authoritative planning context, but must investigate beyond stored mappings when risk, ambiguity, conflict, low confidence, failing tests, runtime contradiction, or locked product intent could affect a decision.

Requirements, manifestation, trace, coverage, and inferred-link operations must never authorize or perform product source-code changes under src/**; every src/** change requires a separate explicit implementation plan and approval gate.
Agent plans and outputs must carry graph-ID grounding before implementation claims are accepted.
Backend-specific derivation of trace governance: every service, API endpoint, mapper, or data contract must trace to an approved requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Data-specific derivation of trace governance: graph records must preserve provenance and validate relationships.
DevOps-specific derivation of trace governance: every CI hook, deployment script, build config, or infrastructure definition must trace to an approved requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Frontend-specific derivation of trace governance: every meaningful React component, hook, store action, or UI behavior must trace to an approved product requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Operations-specific derivation of trace governance: every operational script, monitoring check, alert rule, or runbook must trace to an approved operations requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Security-specific derivation of trace governance: every policy, guard, permission check, or security test must trace to an approved security requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Test/QA-specific derivation of trace governance: every test suite, test case, or quality gate must trace to the acceptance outcome or requirement it verifies, or carry an explicit exemption.
Every meaningful manifestation must trace to an approved requirement or carry an explicit exemption.
Home dashboard: # Active DCXs (status not Approved and not Superseded), # Total DCXs, # Versions. Supabase-sourced; mock contract in HV-1.
Home shows a brand block: favicon + product name at the top of the page.
Home header has a description + Add button that opens the New Version popup/dialog.
The create popup fetches a filtered list of ClickUp tasks from the client API to seed version creation. Backend-deferred: HV-1 satisfies via mock contract.
New Version popup: auto-assigns version number, assigns team members without job titles, optionally attaches reference documents during creation.
ClickUp taxonomy: a ClickUp list = project, a ClickUp folder = client. Backend-deferred: mock contract in HV-1.
One campaign = one DCX with many versions. Adding a version auto-creates the DCX container when none exists. Backend-deferred in HV-1.

Home provides a configurable filter sidebar exposing version properties (Client, Project, Version Status, Created by) as dropdown/select controls; selections persist locally as saved views per REQ-UP-009/010/019.
Home is a fixed full-viewport shell (100vh x 100vw, no page scroll) on desktop; internal blocks scroll within their own regions. Mobile/tablet layout stacks responsively per REQ-RESP-001.
Home shows a read-only logs block of version status-update logs (user name, photo, timestamp), reading the same activity_logs as the builder; auto-loads on page open, no separate log store.
The Home version list is searchable by name, client, or any version property via free-text search.
Home uses a single compact island/shell, not the builder multi-island system; strict islands remain builder-only. No src/builder/ island imports on Home.
Home shows a user block reusing builder header user controls minus the Save action. No Save button present on Home.
Home shows a version list (cards or bento layout) with proper version data; clicking a card navigates to /version/:id per REQ-VER-ROOM.

Reusable interaction feedback & motion system — The app needs one reusable interaction-feedback system so visual quality is consistent and motion never owns business state. — Modular, reusable effects for: valid drop-target highlight, invalid-drop feedback, parent-container glow, selected-card highlight, newly-created-item highlight, focus highlight, collapsed-container expansion animation, drag feedback, save/sync feedback, locked/disabled feedback. Effects must be modular, not hardcoded per card/island. Motion must never own business state or block save/load.

Kanban Builder Island as creation surface — The Kanban Builder Island is a strategic creation surface. It starts compact and expands into a creation/control strip, with a reserved AI entry point, giving users a guided drag-to-create experience. — Compact button expands inline into a creation/control strip. Far-right reserves an AI Chat entry point. Supports drag-to-create: phase created only on drop into a valid board location, action only on drop into a phase, task only on drop into an action. Valid parent/drop target is highlighted during drag; created item animates/highlights after drop.
Ctrl+A selects all eligible builder cards.
Ctrl+C copies the current builder selection.
Ctrl+V pastes copied builder items into a smart target.
Delete and Backspace remove selected builder items through governed deletion rules.
Escape deselects the current builder selection.
Ctrl+S triggers manual save without bypassing autosave governance.
Keyboard shortcuts are guarded while typing into text inputs.

App-wide skeleton loading states — Every app surface that loads data or async content (Builder shell/stage/cards, Home, Version pages, modals, lists, editor panels) must render a proper React skeleton/placeholder that matches the final layout during load, instead of spinners, layout shift, or blank states. This complements REQ-VR-003 (Builder progressive load sequence, backend-scoped ordering) but is broader: it applies app-wide and mandates skeleton placeholders as the loading affordance. Skeletons must respect prefers-reduced-motion (no infinite shimmer when reduced) per core.md §20.
Company Google/Gmail authentication — Users must authenticate using company Google/Gmail authentication. — Auth provider, route guards, user session, backend user
Non-authenticated access — Non-authenticated users cannot open the Builder. — Route guard, no-access screen, login redirect.
DCX/workspace-level access — Access is controlled at DCX/workspace level, not per version. — Workspace user table/model, access checker, version route guard.
Shared on one version = access to all versions — If a user is shared on one version, they have access to — Access resolver, version query permissions, backend policy.
Prefilled workspace users — Project payload should come with prefilled users. — Project payload, creation form, workspace user initialization, backend
Add users — Any user with access to the DCX can add more users for — User management UI, workspace user update endpoint, access
Remove prefilled users — Prefilled users from project payload cannot be removed by normal users. — Workspace user editor, protected user flag, backend validation.
Shared user capability — For MVP, any shared DCX user can do everything inside that DCX — Permission checker, UI action guards, backend policy.
Read access — Any shared DCX user can read all versions under the DCX. — Version list/query permissions, builder route guard.
Edit access — Any shared DCX user can edit non-Ready versions. — Builder edit guards, status checks, autosave permissions.
Ready version permission — No normal shared user can edit a Ready version. — Status-based edit guard, disabled controls, backend save rejection.
Duplicate Ready version — Shared DCX users can duplicate Ready versions into new editable versions. — Version duplication endpoint/service, import engine, logs.
JSON backup actions — Shared DCX users can export/import backups for versions they can access/edit. — Backup import/export utilities, validation before import, permission guard.
Mark version Ready — Any shared DCX user can mark a non-Ready version as Ready if — Status transition endpoint, readiness validation, logs.
Super user concept — Super users will exist and can do anything, but exact definition can — User role model, backend policy, future admin UI.
Role granularity — Detailed roles such as PM, ICS, viewer, approver, admin can be added — Permission schema should not block role expansion.
UI state permission — Permissions should guard real data actions, not local UI behavior like island — Island hooks, builder store, local UI state.
Permission enforcement location — Frontend should hide/disable invalid actions, but backend must enforce access and Ready — Route guards, API middleware/policies, service error handling.
No-access behavior — If user is not authenticated or not shared on the DCX, show — Builder loader, access query, no-access screen, error reporting.

Focus/presentation drill-in — focusing or presenting a builder object expands the object's DESCENDANT subtree (children + grandchildren: a phase expands its actions and their tasks; an action expands its tasks) and centres it in the stage (per REQ-FP-CMA-003). This corrects the current behavior where StageProvider.enterPresentationMode (and useStageExpansion) expand only the target's ANCESTORS and collapse the subtree — the opposite of the intended drill-in. Keep it minimal: expand descendants + centre; do NOT add ancestor/unrelated-collapse complexity. Implemented/fixed in WM-5.

Readiness as shared card-system feature — Readiness should be a shared feature across all cards, but readiness means something different at each level. Cards must never disagree about whether something is ready. — All cards display readiness state. Phase readiness depends on child actions/tasks; action readiness depends on child tasks; task readiness depends on required fields and explicit Not Needed values. Cards must receive readiness from centralized validation/readiness helpers and must not compute it independently.

Per-field readiness states on tasks — Each important task field needs a clear readiness state so the Task Card and Editor agree on what is filled, unset, or intentionally not needed. — Define field-level readiness states (ready / filled / unset / not-needed) for date, missing data, specs, sender, receiver, channel, subtasks. States are computed centrally and consumed by the Task Card indicators and the Editor.

Responsive layout across screen sizes (NOT fixed at one width). (a) The Builder must scale fluidly across desktop-class screens from a 14-inch MacBook (~1280–1512px) up to an 85-inch TV (4K, ~3840px): the frozen three-row structure and island roles (core.md §10) are preserved, but dimensions, spacing, and typography scale via responsive units (clamp/min-max/rem/%), honoring the §21 density discipline (≤260px repeating card columns) at the small end. The Builder is NOT required on mobile/tablet (desktop-and-bigger only). (b) The Home and Version pages must additionally be fully responsive on mobile and tablet. The CT-2 structural-dimension tokens (--dim-*) are the substrate to make fluid. This corrects the discovery's fixed-1440 interpretation; the original intent — and REQ-FP-D12's own note 'aligns with D-01 responsive direction' — was responsiveness. Does NOT change layout structure (§10) or the density discipline (§21); it makes sizing fluid within them.
Nothing is promoted to staging or production without an explicit, recorded PO approval; the promotion path mechanically refuses absent the approval record.
Release governance is enforced mechanically (GitHub rules, Vercel behavior, env separation, CSV registry, approval records, automated checks) rather than by AI prompt discipline.
Branches follow a fixed naming model tying git identity to the session (agent/<session>/<topic>, docs/<session>/<topic>), enforced mechanically.
Parallel agent/dev sessions never cause version conflicts, broken logs, overwritten records, accidental promotions, or preview confusion.
A CSV release registry tracks versions, change class, commit, deployment id, preview/staging/production links, approvals, and related work.
A source-code change increments Iteration and creates a preview deployment.
Every log entry carries its 4-part version and a source vs non-source class; existing historical logs are preserved unchanged (the version field is additive).
Each preview maps one-to-one commit to deployment id to preview URL to branch to session in the registry, with no ambiguity.
Markdown remains human documentation while the structured CSV registry is the operational record of record for releases.
Non-source changes (docs/planning/governance) produce no preview deployment.
The PO solely owns Major and Stage; the system/agents own Iteration and Revision under mechanical rules (CODEOWNERS-locked).
GitHub, Vercel, Supabase, ClickUp, and Google Apps Script each own a defined mechanical responsibility in the release workflow.
Every source-code change automatically produces a preview/test deployment (immutable per-commit URL).
A PO-approved build is promoted to the production URL by re-aliasing the exact immutable deployment, never a rebuild.
Promotion to Staging increments Stage and resets Iteration and Revision to 0; promotion to Production increments Major and resets Stage, Iteration, Revision.
A non-source change increments Revision and creates no preview deployment.
The release machinery seeds safe multi-agent collaboration via a mechanical coordination substrate (CI merge-time version assignment) not dependent on markdown or token memory.
A PO-approved build is promoted to a stable staging URL by re-aliasing the exact immutable deployment, never a rebuild.
The project uses a 4-part version vMajor.Stage.Iteration.Revision; bootstrap v0.3.5.0 carries the existing version as-is.
Ready validation scope — When a version is marked Ready, the system validates the full version — Readiness checker, validation service, useBuilderNodes, status update flow,
Save vs Ready validation — Incomplete work can be saved. Strict validation only blocks switching to Ready. — Autosave logic, validation layer, save status, version status
Ready status lock — Once a version is marked Ready, it becomes locked/read-only and cannot be — Version status logic, route guard, edit permissions, card
Post-ready changes — If changes are needed after Ready, the user duplicates the Ready version — Version duplication service, create version flow, version status
Ready as final MVP state — For MVP, Ready is the final locked state unless a later approval/review — Version status enum, status transitions, UI labels.
Empty phase rule — A phase can exist while planning, but a Ready version cannot contain — Phase validation, status transition checker, phase cards.
Empty action rule — An action can exist while planning, but a Ready version cannot contain — Action validation, action card state, status transition checker.
Task parent requirement — A task cannot exist without an action. — Task creation flow, CreatorIsland, builderMutations, task validation, API
Required task fields — If a task is added, all required task properties must be filled — TaskEditor, task validation, readiness checker, API payload.
Missing data field rule — The missing datafield must either have content or be explicitly marked Not — TaskEditor field model, task type, validation, API DTO,
Specs field rule — The specs field must either have content or be explicitly marked Not — TaskEditor field model, task type, validation, API DTO,
Empty vs intentional none — For fields where “none” is acceptable, the data model should distinguish empty/unanswered — Task schema, validation, UI controls, API mappers.
Draft validation display — In Draft/In Progress, validation issues should be visible as incomplete/warning states, not — Card badges, TaskEditor warnings, validation summary, readiness panel/island.

Common card interaction layer — Phase, Action, and Task cards are strategic Builder components, not simple visual cards. They must share one consistent interaction layer so the whole Builder behaves predictably and can be visually redesigned without rewriting logic. — Provide reusable card primitives + typed card configuration covering: selection, multi-selection, expand/collapse, drag-and-drop, reorder, delete, contextual actions, locked/read-only state, readiness state, creation/selection highlight, parent/drop-target highlight, and motion feedback. Common behavior must not be duplicated per card type.

Card-specific movement rules — Each card type moves differently and that movement is part of its meaning. The shared system must allow per-card movement rules rather than forcing identical behavior. — Phase cards move horizontally across the Kanban stage. Action cards move vertically inside a phase and horizontally between phases. Task cards move inside actions, between actions, and across dates in Timeline. Movement rules are card-type configuration, not shell logic.

Phase Card as strategic visual container — The Phase Card is the highest-value visual container. Beyond holding actions/tasks, it should communicate where the work and focus of a phase concentrates, especially when collapsed, seeding future workload/communication-flow views without a full analytics engine. — Phase Card supports expanded/collapsed states, phase-level task/action density, readiness/blocker indicators, visual emphasis where most work exists, subtle background/light-intensity signals, and a seam for future heatmap and communication-flow indicators.

Action Card as simpler structural component — The Action Card should rely on the shared card system without becoming overdesigned. It is a structural grouping, not an intelligence surface. — Action Card supports selection, expand/collapse, drag/drop, reorder inside phase, move between phases, delete, task count, empty-action warning, readiness state, and locked state. No heatmap/visual-intelligence layer now.

Task Card as field-status surface — The Task Card is a complex, high-investment component. It should communicate at a glance whether each key property is ready, unset, filled, or marked not needed, and allow quick inspection/correction without always opening the full Editor. — Task Card shows compact field indicators for: date/communication-date status, missing data, specs, sender, receiver, channel, subtasks, and future structured properties. Clicking an indicator opens a controlled popup showing field name, current value, readiness state, 'Unset' if not ready, or the selected value. Double-clicking the popup switches it to edit mode.
Manual deselect works through Escape and empty-stage click.
Multi-select copy and paste duplicates selected shared builder cards.

Subtask copy & paste — A user can copy one or more Subtasks and paste them onto the same Task or another Task, duplicating the Subtask instances (definition reference, specs, and field values) without re-running Channel/ChannelComposition generation. This is distinct from REQ-SBC-DUP-001, which duplicates Phase/Action/Task shared builder cards; Subtasks are field-level instances inside a Task (see REQ-SBC-005), not shared builder cards, so card-level copy-paste does not cover them. Paste must go through normal validation and must not blindly overwrite existing subtasks without review.

JSON export backup — Allow user to export a version backup/summary JSON as a personal safety — Affects export utility, version serializer, User Island/file action.

Backup vs official version history — JSON backup is a personal safety backup, not official version history. — Affects backup metadata, activity logs, version history model.
Future collaboration seed — Do not build real-time collaboration in MVP, but seed future readiness through — Affects data fields like updatedBy, updatedAt, logs, future

Reusable sticky resizable popup shell — A single reusable popup shell should serve all floating helper surfaces so they look and behave consistently and never disturb the stage layout. — Reusable sticky, resizable, minimizable popup shell shared by AI Chat, View Helper Island, Google Docs/file preview, advanced Focus filter, and future template browser. It floats over the stage and does not resize the stage. Supports minimize, resize, sticky positioning, branded header, close behavior, and optional saved size/position as a user workspace preference.

Stage as first-class shared system — The Stage should be a first-class product component shared by all views, so Kanban, Timeline, Weekly, and Monthly are renderers inside one Stage rather than unrelated screens. — The Stage owns canvas boundaries, view switching, stage movement, week/month navigation, edge scrolling, off-stage dropzones, layout response to islands, transition feedback, and selected/focus context preservation. Kanban and Timeline are different renderers inside the same Stage concept.

Context-preserving view switching — Switching views should not feel like a hard reset; the workspace should keep useful context so users do not lose their place. — View switching preserves useful context where possible: selected task, focus state, expanded containers, and stage position. Transitions provide feedback rather than reloading the workspace.

Stage + island layout contract — Different surfaces affect the Stage differently, and this must be explicit so future components do not break the canvas. — Explicit layout contract: Editor/Viewer Island reduces/pushes the stage (no overlay); sticky popup shell floats above the stage (no resize); Focus Island filters/isolates visible data (no mutation); View Helper popup floats above the stage and bridges opposite-view logic. Future components must declare which contract they follow.

Kanban movement & off-stage dropzones — Users dragging items in Kanban must never lose drag context when the target is outside the visible area. — Kanban movement: phases horizontal; actions vertical within a phase and horizontal between phases; tasks within and between actions. When dragging near/beyond visible bounds, support edge-scroll, valid insertion zones, off-stage dropzones, and continuation feedback (e.g. moving a phase beyond bounds shows a valid insertion area or scrolls; moving an action to a far phase triggers horizontal movement/target highlight; moving a task to an action highlights the destination).

Timeline movement & off-stage date dropzones — Timeline planning must support dragging tasks to dates outside the visible range without forcing the user to cancel and restart. — Timeline movement: next/previous week or month moves the stage in a controlled way; dragging a task toward a date outside the visible range exposes edge navigation or valid outside-date dropzones; dropping on a valid date updates the task's date context.

Contextual object template popup (seed) — Phase, Action, and Task cards should support a contextual template popup so users can later load object-relevant templates without a full template database in MVP. — Holding/clicking an object opens a contextual popup loading templates relevant to that object type, supporting categories, search, loading state, empty state, and object-aware results. Future apply: phase templates add/merge actions+tasks; action templates add/merge tasks; task templates fill fields/specs/subtasks. Template application must not blindly overwrite without review.

The Version page shows campaign collaborators (all users shared on any campaign version) in the header and the active version's assigned crew in the version control component.
The version control component lists the active version's attached documents/resources, openable safely via external links.

The Version page header shows client name, project name, product type (read from metadata/ClickUp task custom field for now; dedicated endpoint deferred to backend decision), and the derived campaign status.

The version control component launches the full Builder via route navigation (/builder/:id) through a prominent branded launch component (more than a button); no builder visual or src/builder/** import on the Version page (core.md §13, D-5 Option B).

The Version page is a fixed full-viewport shell (100vh x 100vw, no page scroll) on desktop, matching Home. Mobile and tablet viewports stack and scroll internally. The page is fully responsive (mobile + tablet + desktop).

Version Room — Clicking a version card on the Home page navigates to /version/:id (the Version workspace page), not directly to the Builder. The Version page is a dedicated workspace to review metadata, manage status transitions, view resources, crew, and structure, and launch into the Builder. The Builder is only reachable via the Version page (or direct link).

The version control component shows counts of phases, actions, and tasks (read via the builder-tree query as data-only, no builder UI/store import); hovering a count shows a popup listing those items. This is new vs v0.1.4 and carries the 'what's inside' weight under D-5 Option B.

The Version page lists all sibling campaign versions with status; selecting one switches the version control component's context to that version, allowing inspection of each sibling's docs, crew, and structure without leaving the page.

View Helper Island cross-view bridge — The View Helper Island bridges Kanban and Timeline so users can plan across both without switching context manually. — In Timeline view it shows a Kanban-style helper (zoomable, visually similar to main Kanban); tasks with set communication dates are disabled; undated tasks can be dragged into a timeline day, and dropping onto a day links/assigns the task to that campaign day. In Kanban view it shows a weekly timeline helper; scheduled tasks appear by week/day; dragging a task from the helper into an action changes the task's phase/action while the date remains unless changed. Uses the shared sticky/resizable/minimizable popup shell.
